Global SOP DHL Transport Label Specifications

The DHL Transport Label is a label which is printed and attached to a piece
and contains all handling-relevant information i.e. the one required to ship a
piece from a Shipper to a Consignee. It includes piece/shipment
identification, address information and DHL handling information.
This generic title includes:
• ESS Label (includes all information, mostly a Customer Produced
Document as part of the Shipment Preparation Process, requiring no
DHL re-labeling). It also includes specific sections for Customers to
capture their bespoke information.
• Network Label -> transport label without addresses and without
customers’ own segments
• Routing Label -> small label containing DHL Handling Information only
and used by DHL to complement information provided by customers.

A document type reflecting agreed “conditions of contract of transport”
A Waybill is, by definition, NOT a label.
This doc-type was historically called “Archive Doc” and later “Waybill Doc”.

Business term used as a reference for DHL's own Waybills doc-type (also
named “Manual Waybill”).
The term “Multiply Waybill” denotes both the empty and filled-in forms.
Term for a 1:1 replacement of a (damaged) label by a new one

Term for tagging a package with a new label with additional or changed
supplementary information, e.g. Manual Sort Information.

A Unique Identifier (code) assigned to a single transport unit by its issuer, in
accordance with ISO/IEC 15459-1. It is the Leading Industry Standard
Worldwide and acts as primary Piece Identifier (PID) standard for DHL
Express. As such, DHL’s equivalent business term is “Piece Identifier”.

Generic term for DHL Express identifiers at shipment level. Currently
implemented with the 10 NUM format (9 digits, plus 10th digit calculated with
MOD7). Other terms used within DHL are:
• Air Waybill number (Air Express)
• Waybill number (Air and Road Express)
• Shipment number (Road Express)
• Consignment number (Road Express, Freight), when the format
matches the standard 10 NUM with MOD7 check-digit, defined
above.

A technique used in documents, whereby white characters are printed on a
black background, as a visual aid to highlight an important section or piece of
text.
Fixed day delivery (day of the month)

Equal to or greater than 10 kg or 22
lb but less than 20 kg or 44 lb

Equal to or greater than 20 kg or 44
lb

header:
Reference Guide
Global SOP Network Operations
The DHL ISO Routing Code contains destination country, destination Postcode, transport products and
associated product features. When a routing field is optional, the content is not required.
In the context of the routing barcode, optional does not always imply that the routing field may remain
empty. Some routing fields must always be populated at least with zeroes. If data is available, though,
such a field should always be filled.
The Postcode, the region specific fields and the + separator after the product/feature block are the only optional
fields which must be omitted if the data is not available.
5.15.1. Routing Barcode Structure
The following international ISO standards are supported when coding routing information in barcodes.
• The ASC MH 10 routing barcode starts with the ASC MH 10 Data Identifier "2L" and is coded in barcode
symbology Code 128.
• The GS1 routing barcode starts with the GS1 Application Identifier "403" and is coded in barcode
symbology GS1-128.
Both standards are complementary and can co-exist without restriction (they do not differ in content but only in
their technical structure). However, DHL Express-internal applications will only produce ASC MH 10
routing barcodes starting with 2L.
A distinction has to be made between the information which will be captured and the information, which will
actually be printed out on the barcode. This distinction is mainly relevant for applications which need an
indication on the output side.
Example: At data content level, a calendar date for delivery is not chosen for the selected product, so the
delivery date information will not be captured. The delivery date is optional, as it does not always have to be
captured. However, at barcode level, the delivery date digits are mandatory as the delivery date field may not
remain empty/blank and the field must be populated with zeroes if no valid information is available.
All routing information appears both in human and bar-coded format except for the parenthesis
around the Data Identifier which only appear in human readable format – not in the barcode.
The printout level is either mandatory or optional, it means the whole barcode is either printed or not printed
(with the possible exception of a country-specific extension).
A product/feature block always consists of 8 digits, regardless of which products and features have
been chosen.
The table below details out ASC MH and GS1 barcode routing structures

header:
The minimum height for the barcodes is 25 mm, unless formally approved
(Global SOP) differently.
The Routing barcode, may exhibit an exceptional min height of 11 mm,
recommended for all Label templates where space is a challenge.
Both options are currently available and can be used.
rows:
The minimum narrow element dimension (x-dimension) shall not be less than
0.33 mm and shall also not exceed 0.51 mm for the ISO/IEC 15417 Standard.
The GS1 Standard defines size ranges for the GS1 barcode symbologies, in
the EAN General Specifications. For transport packaging, values within the
interval module width 0.495 mm to 0.660 mm are allowed.
Within the above indicated ranges, the biggest x-dimension should be used that
is supported by the printing device used, while complying with barcode length
and quiet zone specifications.

header:
Linear barcode symbols must be printed with leading and trailing quiet zones.
For Code 128, DHL demands not less than 5 mm. Quiet zones are not part
of the barcode itself, i.e. a barcode of 91 mm needs at least 101 mm space on
the label.
rows:
The linear barcode quality must, as a minimum, conform to “Grade B”, tested
according to ANSI and CEN (EN 1635), Test Specifications for Barcode
Symbols.

header:
The original minimum height for the DHL barcodes is 25 mm.
A new barcode version, approved in 2014, has a height of 11 millimeters and is
especially recommended for all Label templates where space is a challenge.
Both options are currently available and can be used.
rows:
The minimum narrow element dimension (x-dimension) must be between 0.36
mm and 0.425 mm.
DHL demands all barcodes to be picket fence-oriented (standing)

Linear barcode symbols must be printed with leading and trailing quiet zones.
For Code 39, DHL demands not less than 6 mm.

The linear barcode quality must, as a minimum, conform to “Grade B”, tested
according to ANSI and CEN (EN 1635), Test Specifications for Barcode
Symbols.
DHL’s Code 39 Waybill barcodes require the standard start- and stop-char “*”

DHL uses the simple MOD 7 check digit: DHL’s 9-digit Waybill number is
divided by 7, and the remainder forms the check digit, becoming the 10th digit
of the Waybill barcode.
Example: Waybill number 310003290
• 310003290 divided by 7 is 44286184.29
• Rounded off to next integer: 44286184
• 44286184 * 7 = 310003288
• 310003290 – 310003288 = 2 = Check digit

The minimum height for the barcode is 25 mm. However, a barcode height of at
least 28 millimeters is recommended

The minimum narrow element dimension (x-dimension) shall not be less than
0.33 mm. The x-dimension shall not exceed 0.51 mm.
Within this range, the biggest x-dimension has to be used that is supported by
the printing device used, while complying with barcode length and quiet zone
specifications.

Linear barcode symbols must be printed with leading and trailing quiet zones.
For Code 128, DHL demands not less than 5 mm. Quiet zones are not part
of the barcode itself, i.e. a barcode of 91 mm needs at least 101 mm space on
the label.

The linear barcode quality must, as a minimum, conform to “Grade B”, tested
according to ANSI and CEN (EN 1635), Test Specifications for Barcode
Symbols.

header: Data encoded in a QR-Code may include alphabetic characters, numbers, double characters and URLs. The
rows: symbology uses a small area of square modules with a unique perimeter pattern, which helps the barcode
scanner determine cell locations to decode the symbol and extract the data.

header: Implementation of this symbology is based on the International ISO/IEC Standards 18004:2006 and is also,
rows: compliant with the ISO/ IEC 18004:2015 specifications.

header: The QR symbology will be used to encode operational data to be used in the PUD and In-Facility domains, in
rows: the context of the LabelFree scenarios.

1. Addition of additional segment specifications for the so-
called “Highly Compact Label”, for both Consignor’s and
Consignee’s Address segments

2. Update with new GDPR conditions applicable to both
Consignor’s and Consignee’s Address segments

3. More explicit prohibition of Account Numbers (and/ or
related information protected through the GDPR
Regulation) on Transport Labels

4. Introduction of new syntax for the Label Certification
Identifiers (permanent and temporary)

5. Relaxation of requirements on location of customer-
segment, upon explicit approval through DHL Express’
Label Certification Body (now with two options to better
meet customer requirements)

6. Relaxation of specifications for the height of the Routing
barcode (Amazon CR 2017)

7. Addition of a note allowing the relocation of the
customer segment after explicit approval from DHL
Express

8. General Clean-up and update of Titles and Acronyms to
the latest standard (e.g. eWaybill, ESS solutions,
License Plate replaced by Piece Identifier – PID, where
applicable)

9. Update of guideline regarding exceptional scenarios,
where contact details could be allowed to be printed on
the Transport Label, in accordance with the GDPR
regulation. Updates were accordingly added to the
respective illustrating images.

10. Update of guidelines relative to single (i.e. always
matching) “Duty / VAT A/C”. No split between both
accounts since they always match

11. Removal of reference to ACS Label, which is not object
of this guideline, from this section

12. Incoterms removed from Waybill Doc, as per CR
451721

header:
13. Reference Loose BBX Transport Label layout, image,
updated with individual segments’ identification
rows: 14. Removal of reference to outdated section (11.1)

15. Removal of outdated section 5.11.2 “DHL Shipment
Identifier (mandatory)” and corresponding renumbering
of the sub-sequent sections
16. Original images replaced with higher-quality ones
17. Updated images to reflect latest GDPR guidelines

18. Piece Identifier length (i.e. number of characters
following the Data Identifier) of type JD01 made more
clear

19. The US Destination Control Statement (US DCS) was
removed from the Transport and Waybill Doc images
as it is no longer required by the US government. The
US government harmonized the US DCS and only
requires it to be listed on the commercial invoice.

21. “Considerations on Highly-Compact Labels” – final
layout and segments’ dimensional matrix updated

22. “Considerations on 2D-enabled Transport Labels” –
Phase 2A layout and segments’ dimensional matrix updated

23. Addition of “2D Data Matrix – Customer Data” section,
formally introducing the 2D-enabled Transport Label layout

24. Addition of Bi-Dimensional Barcode Symbologies and
symbol attributes
26. Specification of Medical EXPRESS Logo added

27. Correction of the area reserved for the Medical
EXPRESS Logo
28. Sample image updated to correspond to and reflect

with Global SOP
Dictionary and Glossary of
Business Terms
Refers to (for example) Segment 1 of reference document type
Relative to characters or to the number of characters of a string
As per the Global SOP Standard for Classification of Types of Documents
Electronic Shipping Solutions; formerly known as “eCom”

Global Standards One – non-profit organization that develops / maintains
global standards for business, in this context the well-know GS1-barcodes.
Relative to the International Air Transport Association

License Plate. Also known as Piece Identifier.
The license plate is defined in ISO/IEC 15459-1. It is a unique identifier for
transport units. This unique number, regardless of use, shall be specified and
assigned by the label issuer and applied to a transport unit and may provide
access to traceability data regardless of content and destination. This
number shall be valid for the lifetime of the unit it is applied to.

Accredited Standards Committee MH10 - The ANSI committee for standards
for Unit Loads and Transport Packages, including the national standard for
bar code labeling
